speaker: interviewer
text: '96. I'm interviewing Bertha Leverton in London, England. Bertha, you were saying that after school, you went to the cheder. Can you tell me something about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, after the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: After the--

speaker: interviewee
text: First, we went to the [NON-ENGLISH], where we did the-- where we had lunch and we did our homework. And then we played. We had different rooms. There was a silent room for a chess playing. And it was really a most wonderful institution. I don't think we were duly grateful for it, as children never are grateful. And after that, when it finished-- I think about 4:00, half past 4:00-- we went to cheder. Those-- the Orthodox ones went to cheder. We carried on cheder. We had about an hour to an hour and a half. So it was rather a long day when we got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn at the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned Chumash. We learned to read, to write, and stories, history, Jewish history. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went every, every day. We learned to [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was, it was an accepted fact for Orthodox children to go to cheder. Also, I remember in school, we had-- also, we had religious instructions, of course, being a Jewish school. They had to-- they separated the children. Because not all the parents wanted them to have the more intensive learning of the Orthodox children. So they had liberal. And then they had the Orthodox teaching. But that was the only time we were sort of segregated at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child growing up in Munich, what did you understand about the political scene? What was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, until I was 10-- in 1933, I was 10-- we had a very carefree life. But like I said, it carried on fairly carefree for another few years. But it intensified as the laws got tougher. For instance, we weren't allowed to go to the cinema anymore-- not that we could go that often. It was a very rare treat to go to the cinema. And I think I can remember each film that ever I saw in Germany. I mean, I don't think there were more than six or 10 all together. But I became a-- at a very early age, I became an opera fan. It was a wonderful thing. I-- and the opera house in Munich was very near our school. So I think from the age of 14 to Kristallnacht, when everything stopped, I and some others of us, we sort of formed groups. And we would queue up very early in the morning. You could get seats in the [? gods ?] on the day of the performance. At 6 o'clock in the morning, we'd start queuing. That means leaving the house at about quarter to 6:00. And each one could only get two tickets. So we took it in turns. And we got our tickets. And I saw some wonderful productions. Even when we weren't allowed to go anymore, nobody asked us questions. We stood there. And once, we saw a performance. And we didn't know Hitler was there. And there was no interval. That's what I do remember. Everything was kept in darkness. There was no interval that evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I saw-- I saw almost-- I must have seen about 10, 12 operas. And that was truly wonderful. The stage there was very, very advanced-- around, what do you call it? It was called in German a [GERMAN], a circular. It was a moving, a moving scenery. It was really magnificent. And I look back with great pleasure on at least having seen such wonderful operas when we weren't supposed to have.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, I liked-- I was very sporty. That's one thing they couldn't take away from us. And that's why we didn't feel so deprived, really. Because we had our bicycles. By then, most of my friends had bikes. And we'd ride out outside Munich on a Sunday. And we'd go swimming in the lakes. There were lots of lakes around. We'd go away for the whole day. And that's something they couldn't take away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was your understanding of what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really that much. We-- everybody thought-- our parents were stupid as well. They were like ostriches, burying their heads in the sand. And then it was too late. Everybody wanted to leave. And it was much too late then. 1936, the people started to worry. My uncles and aunts who all lived in Munich, or the cousins, they left early. They left for what was then Palestine. They seemed to have been the clever ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember it being discussed at home about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really-- my parents, by the time they took numbers out for America-- I had an aunt, my mother's younger sister, lived in America as my mother's side mainly immigrated to America quite early on, before the '30s. And I remember the last thing, in 1938, my aunt came over from America to Munich. And she brought one visa. And she gave it to-- she could only get one at that time-- to my cousin, Elsie, who lived in Nuremberg. And she-- I think she took her back with her. And all the family, Elsie and her brothers and sister and my uncle Alvin from Nuremberg, they left for America, 1938. So that was arranged. But my parents never got visas, so they couldn't leave. We were actually the only ones left behind. And then in 1938, after the Kristallnacht--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was pretty awful, the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before the Kristallnacht, somebody gave us a warning, I don't remember what it-- who it was, that there was going to be a, a transport sent to Poland-- Polish Jews. That was when they separated. The Germans wanted to get rid of the Polish Jews. And they sent them to Poland. Poland didn't want them. They wanted to send them back. They didn't want them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [? Sponjin ?] on the, on the border became a no-man's land. And camps were established there, populated by the German-Polish, the Polish Jews who lived in Germany. I mean, we, we thought of ourselves as Germans. I mean, we were born there. We spoke the language. I didn't speak Polish. I spoke German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but we had some forewarning. And I remember my younger brother and sister were given for a couple of nights to sleep with friends of mine. They were Hungarians whom they didn't touch then. They slept there. And I and my parents walked all night for two nights running in the [INAUDIBLE]. That means the station, the big station in Munich which was very sophisticated already at the time. It never closed, open 24 hours. And they had things like barber shops there and food places like you get in the big stations here. And you could mingle there with the crowd and not be recognized. And we just-- I remember we were walking there for two nights and after that. So we escaped that roundup of the Poles. And a lot of them did come back again. But many of them didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Kristallnacht-- I remember going to school and seeing the school burning in the morning. It was just-- it was just horrific. The school and-- our school was next door to one of the synagogues, to the German-Jewish synagogue. Next door to the school and next door to the [NON-ENGLISH], it was all destroyed. It was a horrific experience for children. Of course, then we went home. And the business was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember I happened to be there when my parents didn't go to the business. And I went. And I went in. And I remember the Nazis, they came to the business. And they were closing it down. They said, you better go home. We're closing down the business. So I said, I've got my school books here. Can I take them with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did use-- I used to do my school-- homework in there, I said. So they said, yes. So I opened the drawer. And I took all the business books and shoved them-- [LAUGHS] and shoved them in my school satchel. Because they had all the names of the customers. I was bright-- well, I was already 15 then. I was 15. I was bright enough to realize that you don't leave any names lying about. So-- and then they closed the business. And they told me to go to the Jewish old age home, which had lost its workers, and to do some work there, which I did. I went every day until the day I left for England. I worked in the Jewish old age home, helping, cooking, cleaning, whatever they wanted me to do. And there were two Nazis in charge. But the proper staff had left. And the poor old people, they were so very confused. And my parents just stayed home. I don't seem to remember actually what we did. They couldn't go to work anymore. The banks were not closed then. You could still get your money out if you had any.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, I remember asking the Nazis, what shall we do with the parcels of laundry which were to be delivered? He can't have been quite that inhuman. So he said-- I remember he said to me, let your father come-- we had a telephone there-- let your father come and pick them up. And he can deliver them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember before they closed it, my father came. And he took stacks of parcels ready for delivery. And he managed to deliver them. And that helped quite a bit with the money as well. And one of our customers had committed suicide. And there was great confusion. And we-- I, I went with him when we delivered there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people wouldn't accept it, they said. So my father said, what should I do with it? Keep it. Just-- you know? That's something I do remember of the Kristallnacht. And after that, like, I went to help in the old age home. And I remember my sister was the one who got us on the Kindertransport. Can you tell me something about that? How you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now that was very-- in Munich at least, I don't know about other towns-- it was quite hush-hush. There weren't any places. I mean, there were very few places. We didn't know anything about it. There was a Jewish committee-- not a refugee committee. There was a committee for people who needed help. They were very organized, in a way, like the Germans themselves-- very, very organized. And people used to go there when they needed help. And my sister came home. We-- she must have gone to a little friend. My sister was-- at the time, she was nine. And she came home. And she said, oh, she knows a secret. She knows a secret. She mustn't tell anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, of course, I mean, what is a nine-year-old, can't keep a secret. And she said that her friend told her she's going to England. So my father pricked up his ears. And my mother, my mother said, nonsense. That child is talking nonsense, she said. Who can go to England, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father listened. And he asked the questions. And he went round to those people. And they told off their daughter for telling the secret. But he asked information. And they said, yes, they heard that there was a Kindertransport going to England. And their child was on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing my father did, went to the committee. And he told us that at first that they denied it. There was no such thing. They don't know. It's all nonsense, it's all in the air.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was so insistent. He said, if you don't give-- put my children on, he said, I'm going to tell all my friends, like blackmail. And he really got two places for my brother and myself. My sister was too little then. She stayed behind. And truly, we were on the first transport out of Munich, which only consisted of about 20 children. There was several afterwards. But altogether out of Munich, I don't think there were more than 60 to 80 children on the Kindertransport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand what it meant to be on the Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I was already one of the oldest ones. I was 15 at the time. We realized that we were going to, to England. And we all thought it was a bit of an adventure. Our parents had prepared us in that way. And later on-- you know the work I'm doing, I've done for the last few years, is to research on the Kindertransport and my talks about it. And all these things, I found out later, what had really happened and that all the parents promised their children it won't be for long, and especially the little children.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could just tell me what your parents told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents told me that all being well, they will come and join us. And-- or they'll go to America. Or they'll go to Palestine or whatever. And we'll meet again in a few weeks or a few months. And you have a wonderful time in England. You learn a new language and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, I supposed the lords and ladies were marching around the street there. My brother always thought he'd become a lord. And my sister thought she'd become an English lady and dressed up in those beautiful uniforms, the-- because I think it was only two years earlier-- it was 1937-- that we saw photographs of Princess Elizabeth and the little-- and Margaret with their parents when he was-- I think it was 1937-- when the king was crowned, when the other King abdicated, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, the news spread over Germany as well. Must have bought some magazines then. And it interested us very much, reading about all that was going on in England. Of course, we thought we'd would join all the aristocracy in England. And, well, we never did, of course. But we came to England and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- did you have much say in what you packed to bring over?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the suitcase, my parents-- my parents packed the suitcase. It was sort of a specified by the committee, by the Jewish committee, what was to be packed. And it all had to be written down, all, you know, properly itemized. And you couldn't take anything else except that one suitcase, which you were supposed to carry yourself, and a rucksack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that is the question I always ask if I give talks to children. What would you pack in that rucksack that you were allowed to pack yourself? I know what I packed in it. What did you pack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a very avid reader. Now, reading was my passion. I packed one of the most wonderful books. I've still got it. It is this great art treasure, which I had collected. My father was a heavy smoker at that time. And he-- and there were checks in the, in the packs of cigarettes. And I collected those. And the most-- one of the most treasured possessions of my life is in the most magnificent paintings. Now, those-- miniatures-- those were the actual. I can show it to you later. Those are the actual reproductions of miniatures. I think they're quite valuable now. Because a lot of them must have got lost. And I brought that book. That was my special one. I brought one or two other books, and that book my friend gave me, which you will see later on, photographs. I brought my little silver knife, fork, and spoon, which I had for my first birthday. The fork has survived with my name, Bertha, written on it, and one or two little china cups that we used on Pesach, Passover, and little things like that. And those were my treasured possessions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents pack anything that you didn't know about that you later found?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. I had a tiny little-- it was my 15-- I was 15. I was nearly 16. So they bought me by 16th birthday present already a bit earlier. And it was something very rare for people who haven't got a lot of money. It was a little gold watch. I was lucky I didn't lose it on the train when the German guards came through to search. A lot of children lost their possessions. And it was a very treasured possession, gold watch. And I had a little ring bought in the same shops. They were customers. I think my parents made a contract with them for the laundry that they used to give us. And I had a little tiny ring with a tiny little diamond in it. And when I came to England, they told me I couldn't wear it on my left finger. Because that's-- it shouldn't be taken full of engagement ring. I was quite a sophisticated nearly 16-year-old. So they told me to put it on the other-- I lost it in the blitz when we were bombed out in country. But little things like that I took with me and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] writing paper and things like that. But actually, we, we had quite a peaceful journey, not like some of my friends that I hear about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember saying goodbye to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. It was hard, but not unduly so, because we thought it was an adventure. It's-- they, they, they didn't show how sad they were. And my sister, later on, she always said the most important thing for her was my mother shouldn't cry. Because she remembers a lot. She also came to see us off. At first they-- in those days, they were still allowed. I was one of the first transports out from Munich on the 6th of-- we left on the fourth of January, 1939. So that was only a few weeks after the Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my experiences of the Hitler times, the worst of the Hitler times, are not as steep as my sister's, who experienced a lot more, though she is much younger. She has got very definite memories of those days when our home was disbanded, you know, when all the things were sort of more or less given away instead of sold. And my parents moved into the Jewish hospital for safety. They did the laundry then because of their profession. They took over the laundry because the staff had to leave in the Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they was sort of as safe as can be for three-- for two or three years, till 1942. And my sister had to go and live with a, a half Jewish couple. And she went to see my parents after school in the, in the Jewish hospital. But she has different recollections, again. Because things were much tougher for her. I think they reopened the school in a small way, classrooms to teach the children again afterwards. But, for me, there was no more schooling. In fact, my schooling really finished when I-- finished when I was 14. Because that was the time when they finished the [NON-ENGLISH] and we weren't allowed. But then there was something called [NON-ENGLISH] that was twice a week, for a few hours twice a week. That was a general thing for all German children, Jewish or not. And for that, we had two classes. And that was Jewish again in Jewish classrooms. And that was the schooling. Apart from that, I helped my parents in the business from the age of 14 until I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going on to your memories of your journey, what was it like coming over here? How did you-- you went from Munich to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went from Munich through Germany. And we picked up more children on the way. We all went via [INAUDIBLE]. That was the last stop into Holland. Everybody who went by train went via Holland. And that was a fantastic feeling, that we left Germany behind you. You breathed free. I mean, at that age you realize what you are escaping, although not the full horror of it. Because that came later on. But we realized how lucky we were that we got out on the Kindertransport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Holland, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight to England. We stayed in Holland only a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you leave from Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hook of Holland to Harwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember of the actual journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was at night with my friend, [? Ilse, ?] who also came on the first Kindertransport out from Munich. And I remember, I was sick on the boat. The boat was very rocky. It was winter. And I, I was very seasick. We shared a cabin. And apart from that, the journey, the crossing, and in the morning, we had English tea with milk in it, which we'd never had before, and then the arrival in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many of you were on that boat, on the Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: All together, the children, there must have been a few hundred children that we'd collected. There was a whole train full. But the Munich crowd sort of kept themselves to themselves. And there were only 20 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything to identify you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all came with labels. I've given my exhibition away just now. Otherwise, I'd show it to you. We've got a big archive exhibition. We came with the cardboard labels. And I think my number is-- oh, wait a minute-- I've got my number on my, on my entry. Actually, it's in, it's in the book, the number. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There we are. They're all alphabetical order. L, M, here we are. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Leverton-- here we are. My number was 4.300-- no, 1,518. So that shows it's an early numbers. Because the numbers went up to nearly 10,000. And that was my--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. Hold it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was what I looked like when I entered Britain. You can see it's a proper German outfit, proper Munich. Not only German-- that was Munich. That was called a dirndl. Yes, we went to school in that. And yes, that was my number. But that was written on a piece of cardboard in strong black letters. We hated it, especially the older ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to wear it outside. And it wasn't very nice, you know, at that age. And I had a very nice-- my best coat on. And there you were with that label stuck around the coat. And we had to wear it to be identified.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember, getting off the boat and arriving-- and where? Where was it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Harwich.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harwich, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did, what did you feel like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] We got on the double-decker buses. And that was-- everybody stormed upstairs. And then we were so afraid that shouldn't topple over. Because-- anyway, of course, the bus filled up with downstairs as well. But everybody made a beeline for the upstairs. That was the first time we'd been on a double-decker bus.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did it take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took us to the reception center in Dovercourt. It wasn't a very long journey. And everything was so interesting, all the little houses, whereas in Germany, we were used to the big apartment houses. And only very rich people lived in what we called villas. They were the separate, small houses. But most people lived in apartments and flats. It was winter, of course, January.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6th of January-- it was my father's birthday. And I remember, we had a little party before we left, sort of a little birthday party for him on the 4th before we left. And we arrived on the 6th of January, 1939, in Harwich, which was a holiday camp. In fact, it's the camp where they shoot And I think it existed until a few years ago, when it was pulled down. And we went to revisit as a group after the big 50-year reunion I organized in 1989. And we went there. And that, for many people, it was the most traumatic, the most memorable time. Because that was the first time we'd, we'd been to revisit the place of our arrival. And the actual huts still existed. They're still there. Or they were there a few years ago. And a plaque has been put up to commemorate our arrival. We saw to that. The mayor of Harwich saw to that a few years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come to that in a moment. But if we go back to your first visit to Dovercourt, when you actually landed.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite an adventure, like I said. We lived in those little huts.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My sister wasn't with me. My sister came six months later. My friend and I shared a hut. And there were big dining halls. It was sort of a Butlins, a cheaper-- you know, a bit cheaper than Butlins. There was a big dining hall where we were made welcome. And on a Saturday and Sunday, the weekend, we all called it the cattle market. People used to come from all over England and view us with a view of taking us to their homes and start a new life. And we should have been very grateful. But we didn't feel very grateful, because it wasn't Very nice to be just picked and chosen by-- at random. And then a lot of children who weren't chosen, they were allocated homes to various people all over Britain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't easy. It wasn't an easy time.
